--- SOURCE 1: Why Sustainable Plastics are Necessary for Supply Chains ---
URL: https://www.plasticsindustry.org/articles/why-sustainable-plastics-are-necessary-for-supply-chains/

SUMMARY:
Reduction of Waste. Within supply chains, recycling and reusing plastics are important measures for minimizing waste and promoting sustainable practices.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: The world of plastic waste: A review - ScienceDirect.com ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042

SUMMARY:
Plastic wastes in environments and its impacts Plastic waste management Recently, plastic waste has gained more attention compared to other types of waste, due to its detrimental impacts on the environment and humans (Al-Mosawi et al., 2017, Sokolova et al., 2023). In addition, this article systematically reviewed the characteristics and uniqueness of plastic, classification, waste generation, environmental and public impacts, disposal methods, and rules and regulations for plastic waste management. The world of plastic waste: A review
====================================

Author links open overlay panel P.G.C.Nayanathara Thathsarani Pilapitiya, Amila Sandaruwan Ratnayake 1

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clema.2024.100220](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clema.2024.100220 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2772397624000042&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •We systematically reviewed up-to-date information on plastics and plastic waste. 
*   •It covers applications, benefits, production, consumption, and classifications of plastics. 
*   •We also discuss the major issues of plastic waste on the environment and human health. 
*   •This article includes process-oriented practices in plastic and plastic [waste management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/waste-management "Learn more about waste management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •It also covers legal frameworks and policies for sustainable environmental management. 

Abstract
--------

People discover various materials from time to time that break the boundaries of traditional materials. Plastic is a revolutionized material, and is referred to as “a material with 1,000 uses”. This review summarized up-to-date research on plastic and its waste pollution. Plastic has a domain throughout human life with its versatile properties such as lightweight, high durability, flexibility, and low production cost. This article describes the applications, benefits, production, consumption, and classifications of plastics. Plastic commercialization began with the Second World War and grew all over the world within less than a century. The global annual production of plastic is more than 359 million tons. Despite all the benefits, plastics cause severe environmental and public health issues. Accordingly, this study addresses the major issues of plastic waste on the environment and human health. Plastics can degrade into micro to nano sizes, and those fine particles are more spreadable in air, water, and soil. Therefore, both terrestrial and aquatic animals go through various negative impacts such as ingestion, entangling, ulcers, low reproduction, and oxidative stress. Microplastics also degrade human health due to cardiovascular diseases, chronic kidney disease, birth defects, cancer, etc. The closing contains the developed end-of-life options (e.g., recycling and reprocessing, incineration with energy recovery, modification reuse, value addition, and landfilling) of biodegradable and non-biodegradable plastic wastes. Several international, regional/national level legislations and policies/concepts (e.g., plastic trade, 3R policy, and circular economy) are available to manage plastic and plastic waste generation. Plastic waste management is also discussed offering practical insights and real-world scenarios. Solutions and challenges in effective plastic waste management guide to create a more sustainable and environmentally responsible approach. Finally, this review article highlights the importance of judicious decisions and the involvement of all stakeholders to overcome the plastic waste crisis.

Keywords
--------

Plastic waste management

Environmental impacts

Marine pollution

Recycling

Circular economy

1. Introduction
---------------

The world has witnessed significant changes through past centuries due to incredible inventions such as plastics. The statement “plastic is the greatest finding in the millennium”, is undeniable. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry, plastic is a “polymeric material that may contain other substances to improve performance and/or reduce costs”. The first plastic was invented in 1860 and the beginning of the global plastic industry was marked in 1907. However, industrial development happened in the 1920s, and plastic production expanded in the 1940s ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#f0005)). In 1950, plastic production was around 2 million tons, and it was boosted to 368 million tons in 2019 ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#f0005)). Plastic consumption has thus increased about 180 times from 1950 to 2018. The worldwide plastic production is calculated to be 400.3 million tons in 2022. Plastic production is thus expected to increase exponentially hereafter ([Rafey and Siddiqui, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0540)). Organic compounds such as synthetic or semi-synthetic substances (petrochemical, partially natural substances) have been used to form soft and flexible plastics. Typically, plastics can be categorized as ‘bio-based/organic polymers’ or ‘engineered’. Engineered plastics are developed using unrefined petroleum, flammable gas, and coal. In addition, about 4% of [fossil fuel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is used to produce plastics. Sustainable raw materials such as carbs, vegetable fat/oils, [microorganisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/micro-organism "Learn more about microorganisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and other natural substances are also used to produce bio-based plastics ([Verma et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0740), [Alhazmi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0015)).

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2772397624000042-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (1MB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2772397624000042-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (1MB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2772397624000042-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Graphical representation of [solid waste](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/solid-waste "Learn more about solid waste from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") composition, important chronological events, plastic production by region, global plastic production and consumption, and per capita plastic waste generation.

Urbanization, economic development, and increasing population are the main drivers for the growth of plastic waste ([Amasuomo and Baird, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0030)). Waste can be generally declared as useless by-products/unwanted substances that are generated mainly during human activities and cause egregious results for both the environment and humans ([Amasuomo and Baird, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0030), [Hossain et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0265), [Hossain et al., 2022b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0270)). Meanwhile, the United Nations (UN) defined waste as “Materials that are not prime products for which the generator has no further use in terms of his/her own purposes of production, transformation or consumption, and of which he/she wants to dispose of”. Even though waste generation has a long history, real problems are exposed after losing the waste absorption ability of the environment. Waste can be classified into several categories based on its physical state (solid, liquid, gas), use, [material type](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/materials-class "Learn more about material type from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (glass, paper, wood), safety level, and so on. Physical state is the most popular waste classification among these categories. [Solid wastes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/solid-waste "Learn more about Solid wastes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as paper, glass, food, plastic (ca. 10%), and metal are more frequently concerned in the community than other substances ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#f0005)). Recently, plastic waste has gained more attention compared to other types of waste, due to its detrimental impacts on the environment and humans ([Al-Mosawi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0020), [Sokolova et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0650)).

Discarded plastic debris can accumulate in various natural habitats. Therefore, plastic waste pushes not only the environment but also humans into a critical stage. After being exposed to [ultraviolet radiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/ultraviolet-radiation "Learn more about ultraviolet radiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), plastic debris undergoes a photo-oxidation process, causing it to become brittle ([Gunawardhana et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0230)). The fragmented plastic circulates in different environments and as a result, numerous species are affected by both its chemical and physical impacts. The first encounter of plastics in wildlife, plastic debris in the guts of seabirds, was reported in 1960 ([Suaria et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0670)). The current evidence indicates a growing impact related to public health issues arising from the present use of plastics. [Plasticizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/plasticizer "Learn more about Plasticizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [flame retardants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flame-retardant "Learn more about flame retardants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [stabilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/stabilizer-agent "Learn more about stabilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), antioxidants, and other additives are added during the manufacturing of plastics to gain specific properties. Consequently, harmful chemicals such as [phthalates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/phthalate "Learn more about phthalates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), bisphenol A (BPA), and [polybrominated diphenyl ethers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/pbde "Learn more about polybrominated diphenyl ethers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PBDEs) can be directly transferred to the human body from plastics.

We summarized, analyzed, and synthesized existing research on particular topics such as plastic, plastic waste, environmental impacts, and waste management. This article differentiates itself from other existing works in this field (e.g., [Khoaele et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0365), [Kibria et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0370), [Tiwari et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0705)), along with an exploration of the efficacy of current policies and incorporating technological advancements in recycling and waste management. In addition, this article systematically reviewed the characteristics and uniqueness of plastic, classification, waste generation, environmental and public impacts, disposal methods, and rules and regulations for plastic waste management. Therefore, this study investigated the collective global initiatives aimed at fostering a plastic-free world. By scrutinizing these diverse aspects, this review aims to provide a nuanced understanding of the multifaceted challenges posed by plastic waste, and the concerted efforts underway to address them on a global scale.

2. Plastics
-----------

### 2.1. Plastic production, properties, applications, benefits, and consumption

Humans have passed through several eras, and some of the periods have been named after the materials that made remarkable implements and other necessities, like the Stone Age, Bronze Age, and Iron Age. History has repeated itself, as now we are living in the Plastic Age. The word plastic was coined from the Greek word ‘Plasticos’, which means to be able to form. Plastic is widely known as a [synthetic polymer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/synthetic-polymer "Learn more about synthetic polymer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") developed by polymerization of [monomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/monomer "Learn more about monomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") extracted from [petrochemicals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/petroleum-product "Learn more about petrochemicals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and combined with other chemicals ([Torres-Agullo et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0710)). Monomers are the repeating units of long-chain polymers that are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen held together by [covalent bonds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/covalent-bond "Learn more about covalent bonds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Ethylene and [propylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/propylene "Learn more about propylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are common examples of lightweight monomers that are widely used in plastic manufacturing ([Hassan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0245)). Throughout the polymerization process, certain chemical groups may be omitted from each monomer, resulting in polymers inconsistently preserving the chemical properties or reactivity of the original monomer unit. Polymerization can be ‘addition’ (combining monomers while maintaining their original structure) or ‘condensation’ (monomer structure changes during the process and forms byproducts such as water). Specific chemicals used in plastics vary, depending on the type of plastic manufactured or production process. Plastic production uses several additives like fillers, [plasticizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/plasticizer "Learn more about plasticizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [pigments](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosynthetic-pigment "Learn more about pigments from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), foaming agents, processing aids, lubricants, [heat stabilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/heat-stabilizer "Learn more about heat stabilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), acid scavengers, anti-oxidants, UV [stabilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/stabilizer-agent "Learn more about stabilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [flame retardants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flame-retardant "Learn more about flame retardants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and some [antistatic agents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/antistatic-agent "Learn more about antistatic agents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in different quantities to control different functions like improve properties, ease of processing, increase durability, enhance performance, and appearance.

Presently, the industrial sectors actively explore biomaterials as alternatives to petroleum-based traditional plastic materials ([Khoaele et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0365), [Tiwari et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0705)). These [bioplastics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioplastics "Learn more about bioplastics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") consist either entirely or partially from [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/natural-resource "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as vegetable oils and starches (plant-based products). Bioplastics have the potential to decrease environmental impacts, specifically reducing carbon footprint, and limiting [greenhouse gas emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/greenhouse-gas-emission "Learn more about greenhouse gas emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") associated with polymer utilization. The development of modern bioplastics gained momentum in the 1980s, driven by a desire to minimize landfill waste ([Shah et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0625)). Therefore, the primary objective in the production of bioplastics is to create an [environmentally friendly product](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmentally-friendly-product "Learn more about environmentally friendly product from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that matches the quality of conventional plastics. Even though bioplastics account for only 1% of total plastic production ([Narancic et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0480)), the anticipated expansion of the global bioplastics and [biopolymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biopolymer "Learn more about biopolymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") market is poised to increase from USD 10.5 billion in 2020 to reach USD 27.9 billion by 2025 ([Degli et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0145)).

Plastics have several valuable properties such as being lightweight, toughness, high [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), good stiffness, versatile fabrication, design capabilities, good insulation, a poor conductor of electricity/heat, and [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Ilyas et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0295), [Pan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0515)). Synthetic semi-aromatic plastics (e.g., [polyethylene terephthalate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/polyethylene-terephthalate "Learn more about polyethylene terephthalate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"): PET and [polybutylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/polybutene "Learn more about polybutylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") terephthalate: PBT) have excellent physio-chemical properties such as high heat distortion temperatures, good mechanical strengths, excellent chemical resistance, and electrical insulation. Properties such as high [glass transition temperature](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/glass-transition-temperature "Learn more about glass transition temperature from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Tg) and high melting temperature (Tm) indicate their ability to maintain shape and strength at elevated temperatures ([Wu et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0805)). The low manufacturing cost, [malleability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/malleability "Learn more about malleability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and processing abilities are also important factors in plastic becoming an important material in every human life.

Accordingly, those properties enhance the usage of plastics, especially during the Second World War with the invention of nylon ([Kedzierski et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0350)). Plastics have many ways to shape life by being involved in every field such as [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), construction, transportation, [heat insulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thermal-insulation "Learn more about heat insulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), packing, manufacturing, electronics, furniture, toy and leisure items, automobiles, and medicine ([Oehlmann et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0490), [Pan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0515)). Currently, 85% of [medical devices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/medical-device "Learn more about medical devices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as intravenous bags, disposable syringes, sterile packaging, and joint replacement are manufactured with plastics due to their lightweight and bio-compatibility properties ([Ilyas et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772397624000042#b0295)). The advantages of plastics can be identified as (i) environmental benefits (e.g., PET beverage packing materials reduce energy consumption and greenhouse gas emission compared to glass and metal packing, and lighter plastic composites in aircraft result in low fuel consumption and easy assembly), (ii) [social benefits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/social-benefit "Learn more about social benefits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., supply and storage of clean [drinking water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/potable-water "Learn more about drinking water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by installing a range of diverse water controlling and distribution systems, and food packaging allow safe and time-dependent storage of fresh products in controlling temperature/pressure conditions), and (iii) economic benefits (e.g., re

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://lyrical-cormorant.transforms.svdcdn.com/production/assets/images/Images/Canyon.jpg?w=2000&h=350&auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=crop&dm=1752574623&s=3e13f9da5cc34a1542e1981e76254f3b)

Expert View

28 November 2024

## Trading Our Way Out of Plastic Pollution: The Role of Sustainable Substitutes and Alternatives

### ****There is an urgent need for innovative solutions to address the global plastic pollution crisis. A promising approach is to utilize nature’s renewable resources, including marine-based resources, to develop and scale up non-plastic substitutes and plastic alternatives. Trade can play a key role in this transition.****

****There is an urgent need for innovative solutions to address the global plastic pollution crisis. A promising approach is to utilize nature’s renewable resources, including marine-based resources, to develop and scale up non-plastic substitutes and plastic alternatives. Trade can play a key role in this transition.****

![](https://lyrical-cormorant.transforms.svdcdn.com/production/assets/images/Authors/Lorenzo-Formenti.PNG?w=750&auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=crop&dm=1732783860&s=bbac8b0f92051f4147b95b39365fb239)
![](https://lyrical-cormorant.transforms.svdcdn.com/production/assets/images/Authors/Henrique-Pacini.jpg?w=750&auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=crop&dm=1732783860&s=59171abee06d94668526ceaf85e646a7)

**Publication Date**

28 November 2024

Share this page

## An Erupting Global Crisis

The 20th century was marked by some regrettable technological and industrial choices, such as the use of leaded gasoline or DDT as an agricultural pesticide until bans and controls were later introduced. What was once perceived as remarkable solutions to economic growth and development became societal calamities taking a huge toll on human health and the environment. In recent years, another wonder product, plastics, whose production has grown exponentially, has become the engine of an alarming global crisis; choking oceans, littering beaches, entering food chains, and invading our bodies.

It is estimated that every day the equivalent of [2,000 garbage trucks full of plastics](https://www.unep.org/plastic-pollution) are dumped into the planet’s oceans, rivers, and lakes, disrupting ecosystems and threatening human health. The projections are also bleak. Without action, [stocks of accumulated plastics in the aquatic environment](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/global-plastics-outlook_aa1edf33-en.html)
will more than triple from 140 to 493 million tonnes between 2019 and 2060. While bans on single-use plastics or waste management regulations are a start to addressing the plastic pollution crisis, there is an impelling need for scalable solutions that shake the plastics economy to its foundations and revolutionize material flows influencing production and consumption.

## Natural Substitutes as Part of the Solution

To turn off the tap of plastic pollution, we need to replace plastics with alternative materials that perform the same functions with lower environmental impact. While doing so, we need to open up new production possibilities and markets that—unlike plastics—contribute to regenerating nature. This is not an easy task, but it is possible.

[Non-plastic substitutes and plastic alternatives](https://unctad.org/publication/plastic-pollution-pressing-case-natural-and-environmentally-friendly-substitutes) offer the potential to align environmental goals with socio-economic objectives, especially in developing countries (particularly for substitutes or alternatives produced from agri-residues and other sources that are not intensive in land use).\* For these countries, producing such materials can nurture indigenous capacities, create jobs, and boost exports, thereby capitalizing on their natural resources and waste. This is the case of bagasse, for example, a by-product of sugar production which is widely produced in developing countries and is increasingly seen as a [viable substitute for plastics](https://unctad.org/publication/plastic-pollution-pressing-case-natural-and-environmentally-friendly-substitutes) in packaging and everyday products such as cutlery and bags.

Ranging from algae-based polymers for bioplastics to marine compounds used as fillers in glass and ceramics, marine-based substitutes and alternatives to plastics are of natural origin and extracted from abundant marine resources. For these reasons, they offer unique advantages over terrestrial alternatives.\*\* For example, [seaweed production](https://unctad.org/publication/ocean-opportunities-potential-seaweed-advance-food-environmental-and-gender-dimensions) is not intensive in freshwater or land use, and does not require pesticides or fertilizers. It generates a lower carbon footprint than natural fibres from agriculture, while also providing economic opportunities for coastal communities.

## Early Signs of a Market Movement

A closer look at the data suggests that a shift in material markets towards non-plastic substitutes and alternatives may already be underway. The global market for marine-based substitutes and alternatives, for example, is already worth $10.8 billion and is expanding, driven by the rapid growth of certain segments. For instance, global exports of marine biopolymers, including agar-agar and alginates, grew at an average annual rate of 6% between 2012 and 2022. This is six times faster than their synthetic equivalents (Figure 1). Developing countries play an increasingly important role in these shifts, with some of the main trading hubs located in the developing world. Indonesia, for example, was the world´s largest exporter of seaweed in 2022.

**Figure 1. Global Exports of Marine Biopolymers and Seaweed vs. Synthetic Polymers (Base Year: 2012 = 100)**

![](https://lyrical-cormorant.transforms.svdcdn.com/production/assets/images/Infographics/Plastic-substitites-Figure-1.png?w=1000&auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=crop&dm=1732783850&s=87bcef061d56a5cc74db184978916dd7)

##### Source: UNCTAD. (2025, forthcoming). *Leaving the shore: Marine-based substitutes and alternatives to plastics*.

Source: UNCTAD. (2025, forthcoming). *Leaving the shore: Marine-based substitutes and alternatives to plastics*.

As these figures show, trade can enable the transition to sustainable plastic alternatives. By opening up new markets, it allows producers to scale up their operations. It also facilitates the shift to sustainable materials by making them more accessible in regions where they are scarce. For example, by importing seaweed-based packaging, countries that lack natural resources can reduce their reliance on predominantly synthetic single-use plastics. This simultaneously curbs fossil fuel use in production and pollution from consumption, leading to significant environmental benefits.

The rising interest in sustainable materials is reflected in the increasing number of measures regulating and supporting their trade. UN Trade and Development (UNCTAD) has [examined environmental measures notified](https://unctad.org/publication/beyond-plastics-review-trade-related-policy-measures-non-plastic-substitutes) by World Trade Organization (WTO) members, revealing that measures targeting non-plastic substitutes increased by an average 13% per year over the period 2009–2021; twice as fast as all environmental measures. With paper, glass, and natural fibres being the most targeted materials, the notified measures include both technical regulations and support instruments, ranging from import bans on non-plastic substitutes to direct cash transfers (Figure 2).

**Figure 2. Environmental Measures Targeting Non-Plastic Substitutes Notified to the WTO, by Commodity/Material (2009–21)**

![](https://lyrical-cormorant.transforms.svdcdn.com/production/assets/images/Infographics/Plastic-substitites-Figure-2.png?w=1000&auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=crop&dm=1732783850&s=77402d4baaebaaa30b71306101a2236a)

##### Source: UNCTAD. (2024). *[Beyond plastics: A review of trade-related policy measures on non-plastic substitutes](https://unctad.org/publication/beyond-plastics-review-trade-related-policy-measures-non-plastic-substitutes)*.

Source: UNCTAD. (2024). *[Beyond plastics: A review of trade-related policy measures on non-plastic substitutes](https://unctad.org/publication/beyond-plastics-review-trade-related-policy-measures-non-plastic-substitutes)*.

## Hurdles Along the Way

There are challenges, however, in scaling up non-plastic substitutes, particularly in developing countries where producers can face economic and regulatory hurdles related to market size and capacity constraints. For example, the development of algae-based plastic alternatives can be held back by technologies that are expensive to acquire and require specialized skills. Low production volumes and high costs can also limit the achievement of economies of scale, making these alternatives too expensive to compete with conventional plastics. Consequently, bio-based plastics are sold at a premium, with average prices two to three times higher than for conventional plastics.

Import tariffs and regulations also constrain access to global markets. Although the average most-favoured-nation (MFN) tariffs applied to marine bio-polymers are only slightly higher than those applied to synthetic polymers, the raw materials used to produce many of these alternatives, such as mollusk shells and jellyfish, are subject to tariffs that are up to three times higher (Figure 3). While this creates opportunities for value-added sectors in coastal developing countries, it can restrict access to critical raw materials and strain supply chains.

Figure 3. Average MFN Tariffs Applied to Marine-Based Substitutes and Alternatives vs. Synthetic Polymers Globally, by Material Category

![](https://lyrical-cormorant.transforms.svdcdn.com/production/assets/images/Infographics/Plastic-substitites-Figure-3.png?w=1000&auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=crop&dm=1732783850&s=f465488dc07b10d113a495df1404fcac)

##### Source: UNCTAD. (2025, forthcoming). *Leaving the shore: Marine-based substitutes and alternatives to plastics*.

Source: UNCTAD. (2025, forthcoming). *Leaving the shore: Marine-based substitutes and alternatives to plastics*.

The complexity of regulations is another barrier. For example, many countries still categorize and cover seaweed-based products under food regulations, subjecting them to non-tariff measures that are unnecessary for non-food applications such as packaging. Intended to safeguard human health, sanitary and phytosanitary measures often require certifications that can impose significant financial and technical burdens on exporting firms. Combined with limited harmonization, such standards and measures disproportionately impact small firms, especially in developing countries that lack the financial and technical resources to comply with these measures.

## The Way Forward

To help overcome these hurdles, governments, international organizations, businesses, civil society organizations, and academia need to work together to build a supportive ecosystem for sustainable trade in non-plastic substitutes and plastic alternatives. Here, policy coherence and coordination are key, hence the importance of multilateral frameworks, including those arising from the UN’s [International Negotiating Committee](https://www.unep.org/inc-plastic-pollution) (INC) to develop a global plastics treaty, as well as regional efforts like the [East African Community bill](https://admin.kepsa.or.ke/public/files/docs/17313106502.pdf) on prohibiting single-use plastics. Currently in draft form, the bill aims to establish a harmonized regional approach to tackling plastic pollution by banning non-essential plastic items.

### Governments, international organizations, businesses, civil society organizations, and academia need to collaborate to build a supportive ecosystem for sustainable trade in non-plastic substitutes and plastic alternatives.

Governments should create an enabling environment through supportive policies, market incentives, and infrastructure investments that address key market failures. This includes the harmonization of standards and refined product classifications based on the World Custom Organization’s [Harmonized System](https://www.wcoomd.org/en/topics/nomenclature/overview/what-is-the-harmonized-system.aspx), widely used in international trade. With appropriate support and incentives, businesses can drive innovation and bring substitutes and alternatives to market, from raw material extraction to retail. Academia can advance scientific knowledge and provide expert advice to policymakers and industry. Civil society organizations can shed light on the various dimensions of plastic pollution and advocate for policy and behavioural change.

Policy dialogue that connects multilateral fora with local stakeholders is essential to sustain change and make policies effective. Decisions made in international fora often have direct consequences for local producers, who may not be fully aware of or prepared for these regulatory shifts. Bridging the gap between international decision-making hubs and local supply chain actors can foster a better flow of information, enabling producers in developing countries to participate in and benefit from these policy changes rather than being adversely affected.

These principles are well reflected in the [ongoing negotiations for a global plastics treaty](https://www.unep.org/inc-plastic-pollution/session-5)—a promising example of how multilateral processes can include substitutes as part of a comprehensive global strategy to combat plastic pollution. With reference to “fostering research, innovation, development and use of sustainable alternatives and non-plastic substitutes,” the [INC chair´s non-paper](https://wedocs.unep.org/bitstream/handle/20.500.11822/46483/Non_Paper_3_E.pdf) proposed as a basis for negotiations at the INC´s fifth session in Busan, Republic of Korea, illustrates how the INC can help level the playing field in the global materials market. The INC negotiations also highlight the importance of inclusivity and diversity in policymaking by giving a voice to stakeholders from a broad range of countries and sectors.

In conclusion, the fight against plastic pollution demands well-functioning markets, global cooperation, and inclusive policy frameworks. By promoting sustainable alternatives and substitutes to plastics through trade, plastic pollution can be addressed beyond the use of control measures in ways that foster socio-economic benefits and market innovation.

-----

\* [Non-plastic substitutes](https://unctad.org/publication/plastic-pollution-pressing-case-natural-and-environmentally-friendly-substitutes) are natural materials of mineral, plant, animal, marine, or forestry origin that have properties similar to those of fossil fuel-based plastics. They are typically biodegradable/compostable or erodible in nature and suitable for reuse, recycling, or disposal. [Plastic alternatives](https://unctad.org/publication/plastic-pollution-pressing-case-natural-and-environmentally-friendly-substitutes) are bioplastics derived from biological sources such as biomass ("bio-based plastics") or have biodegradable properties ("biodegradable plastics").

\*\* Polymers are large molecules formed by linking numerous smaller molecules, called monomers. These monomers act as repeating units, creating a long chain-like structure. The specific properties of a polymer (strength, flexibility, etc.) allows them to be the fundamental building blocks of plastics. By varying the monomer and chain structure, a vast array of plastics are produced with a wide range of characteristics and applications.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Public consciousness about plastic pollution is surging, driven by alarming statistics and media coverage, prompting calls for immediate action to mitigate environmental degradation.

As plastic waste accumulates, innovative strategies like circular economy practices are essential to transform waste into valuable resources, fostering sustainable consumption and production patterns.
Upcycling transforms discarded plastics into new products, reducing waste and resource consumption, thus playing a crucial role in sustainable development and pollution reduction efforts.
